g.desc:
A comprehensive blueprint showing all components of the Walker robot separated and floating in space.
Wooden Panels: The two long, curved Spines, the flat Torso and Trunk baseplates, the Front sensor holder, and the four jointed Legs are all detached.
Motors & Hardware: The two Servomotors are shown removed from their square mounting brackets. The Spline Horns (the motor arms) are detached from the motors and the legs.
Small Parts: The black O-ring floats at the top. Numerous screws and hex nuts of various sizes (Types A, B, C, and D) are scattered near the parts they originally secured.
The Feet: The four rubber Grommets are shown pulled away from the circular holes at the bottom of the legs.
The Eyes: The rectangular Range Finder circuit board is separate from its wooden holder.
Tactile Organization & Teardown Tips To wrap up the mission and prepare the kit for the next student (or the next project), follow these steps:
1. Hardware Grouping It is very helpful to sort the screws by size, as they are easily confused:
Longest (Type C): Used for the leg-to-arm joints and securing the head. Medium (Type A): Used for the motor mounts. Tiny/Short (Types B & D): Used for the plastic motor shafts and horn assembly.
2. Rubber Component Care Check the Grommets (feet) for any dust or hair they might have picked up while walking; a quick wipe keeps them grippy. Place the O-ring around one of the wooden panels so it doesn't roll away or get lost in the bottom of the box.
3. Electronics Safety Ensure the Servomotor wires are coiled loosely. Avoid sharp bends near the motor housing where the wires enter the plastic. Place the Ultrasonic Sensor (the "eyes") back into its protective bag. The two metallic cylinders are sensitive to impact.
4. Stacking the "Skeleton" The wooden pieces are designed to lay flat. Stack the two Spines together and the Torso/Trunk plates together to save space.
g.title: Walker: Full Disassembly Diagram

g.desc: Parts list for the Walker Kit
g.title: Inventory

g.desc:
A technical blueprint showing the removal of the sensor unit from the front of the robot.
The Action: Two orange dashed arrows point downward, showing the removal of the Type A screws from the bottom of the baseplate. Above the baseplate, two matching arrows point upward, showing the hex nuts being released.
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "1" is displayed, and the first of five circles is filled in.
Text Prompt: "Remove Screws and Nut."
Tactile & Workspace Tips: Managing Small Hardware: As you loosen the screws from underneath with a screwdriver, the hex nuts on top will become loose. Use a finger to trap them against the wooden frame so they don't bounce away when they detach.
Component Storage: Once the screws and nuts are removed, lift the Range Finder and Holder assembly straight up. This is a delicate component; set it aside in a safe spot (or back in its protective bag) immediately to avoid damaging the sensor "eyes."
Screw Identification: Keep these Type A screws (the 3/8" 4-40 screws) in a separate pile or container. You'll need to distinguish them from the shorter motor screws later on.
g.title: Walker Disassembly: Step 1A

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the removal of the range finder holder from the robot's torso.
The Action: The completed Range Finder and Holder assembly is shown being lifted vertically away from the robot's base.
The Connection: An orange dashed arrow points upward from the square slot in the baseplate, indicating that the bottom tab of the wooden holder is being pulled out of its mounting point.
Text Prompt: "Detach Holder from Walker Torso".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Smooth Vertical Lift: Now that the screws are gone, the wooden tab is the only thing holding the assembly in place. If it feels stuck, do not wiggle it side-to-side, as this can snap the wooden tab. Lift straight up with steady pressure.
Cable Awareness: As you lift the sensor, be mindful of the four jumper wires connected to the back of the Range Finder. Carefully unplug them from the sensor pins or the controller board before moving the holder too far away.
Storing the Sub-Assembly: You can leave the Range Finder attached to the wooden holder with the O-ring for now to keep them together, or proceed to detach them if you are doing a full component breakdown.
g.title: Walker Disassembly: Step 1B

g.desc:
A technical blueprint showing the final breakdown of the ultrasonic sensor unit.
The Action: The black O-ring is shown being expanded and removed from the assembly.
The Separation: Orange dashed arrows indicate that once the O-ring is removed, the rectangular Range Finder circuit board can be separated from the wooden Holder.
Organization Prompt: "Remove O-Ring to detach Range Finder from Holder. Place each item on its spot on the Components sheet".
Tactile & Organization Tips: O-Ring Elasticity: Be careful not to overstretch the O-ring when pulling it off the wooden notches. It needs to retain its elasticity for the next project.
Sensor Protection: The Range Finder's two metallic "eyes" are its most vulnerable parts. Once detached, avoid touching the mesh covers on the front of the sensors.
The Components Sheet: Piper kits often include a 1:1 scale drawing of all parts. Placing the wooden holder, the sensor, and the O-ring directly onto their matching outlines on this sheet is the best way to ensure nothing is missing before you close the box.
g.title: Walker Disassembly: Step 1C

g.desc:
A technical blueprint showing the Walker robot flipped upside down to reveal its underside.
The Perspective: The robot's four legs are pointing upward.
The Action: Two orange dashed arrows point away from the center of the two Servo Horns (the wooden bridges connecting the legs).
The Hardware: Two screws, identified by the letter "B" in a square box, are shown being unscrewed from the very center of the rotating motor shafts.
The Result: Removing these central screws allows the entire leg-and-bridge assembly to be pulled off the motor splines.
Instruction: "Remove center screws to detach leg assemblies from motors."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "B" Screw: These are the specialized screws that threaded directly into the plastic or metal output shaft of the servomotor. They are usually silver and have a distinct pointed tip compared to the machine screws used elsewhere.
Firm Grip: While unscrewing, hold the wooden leg assembly firmly with your other hand. You want the screwdriver to do the work, not the internal gears of the motor. Avoid letting the motor "spin" against the screwdriver pressure.
Pulling the Spline: Once the screw is out, the leg assembly might still feel "stuck." This is just the friction of the tiny plastic teeth (the splines). Pull the wooden bridge straight up and away from the motor body; it should pop off with a small amount of force.
Organization: Keep these two Type B screws separate from the others. If you mix them up with the wood screws, they can strip the motor shafts during the next build.
g.title: Walker Disassembly: Step 2A
Walker Disassembly: Step 2B
g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the removal of a servo motor assembly from the robot's torso.
The Action: The FS90 Servo Motor and its rectangular Servo Holder are shown being lowered vertically out of the torso frame. Two long screws are being removed from the bottom, while two hex nuts are lifted off the top surface.
The Connection: Orange dashed arrows point downward, indicating the exit path for the 3/4" #4-40 Screws (C). Corresponding arrows point upward and away from the top of the plywood torso, showing the removal of the #4-40 Hex Nuts.
Text Prompt: "Uninstall Servo Motors by removing the Screws and Nuts."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Hardware Management: This step uses the longest screws in your kit (Type C). As you unthread them, keep a finger on the hex nuts on the top side of the torso; these are small and can easily roll away once the screw tension is released.
Servo Alignment: The servo motor is sandwiched between the torso frame and the wooden servo holder. When the screws are removed, the motor may drop suddenly. Support the motor from underneath with your palm to prevent it from falling and straining the delicate signal wires.
The Smooth Side Rule: Remember that the etched/smooth side of the plywood components should face outward. If you are disassembling to troubleshoot or re-skin the robot, take note of how the servo holder was oriented against the motor body for easier re-assembly later.

g.title: Walker Disassembly: Step 3
g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the removal of two wooden spines that connect the robot's main body segments.
The Action: Two identical Spines are being lifted vertically away from the Torso (the larger plate on the left) and the Trunk (the smaller plate on the right).
The Connection: Four orange dashed arrows point upward from cross-shaped slots in the baseplates. These arrows indicate that the keyed tabs on the underside of the spines are being pulled out of their respective mounting points on both the Torso and the Trunk simultaneously.
Text Prompt: "Detach the Spines from both the Torso and Trunk."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Bridge" Disassembly: These spines act as the bridge between the two halves of the robot. Because they are slotted into two different plates at once, they can be a bit finicky. It is best to lift both spines together, or alternatingly "walk" them out—lifting the front a few millimeters, then the back—to prevent the wooden tabs from binding in the slots.
Keyed Slots: Notice that the tabs and slots are "keyed" (shaped like a cross or a T). Ensure the spine is perfectly perpendicular to the baseplates before pulling. If the spine is tilted, the tabs will catch on the edges of the plywood, which could lead to splintering.
Orientation Check: As you remove them, take note of the etched lines on the Torso and Trunk. To ensure the robot walks straight when reassembled, the smooth, etched sides of the Spines should be facing outward, away from the center of the robot.

A technical line-art blueprint for Step 4 showing how to detach the legs from the servo horn assembly.
The Action: Two Legs are being removed from the outer ends of a wooden Servo Horn. Two screws are shown being pulled upward from the top of the horn, while two hex nuts are shown sliding out horizontally from the keyed slots in the legs.
The Connection: Orange dashed arrows point vertically upward from the screw heads, which are marked with an "A" icon. Corresponding horizontal arrows show the #4-40 Hex Nuts being removed from the side-entry slots at the top of each leg.
Text Prompt: "Deattach Legs from Servo Horn by removning Screws and Nuts. Repeat 2x with all four legs."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Nut Traps: The legs use a "nut trap" design where the #4-40 Hex Nut is held in a small horizontal slot. These can sometimes be a tight fit. If the nut doesn't slide out easily once the screw is removed, you can use one of the 3/8" #4-40 Screws (A) to gently poke it out from the opposite side.
Screw Identification: This step specifically uses the Type A screws. These are shorter than the ones used for the motor mounts. Keeping these separate from the Type C and D hardware will make re-assembly much faster.
Grommet Care: Note that the bottom of each leg has a Grommet pre-installed. As you handle the legs during disassembly, check to ensure the rubber or plastic grommet is seated firmly and hasn't become loose or cracked.
Component Organizing: Since you are repeating this for all four legs, this is a great time to use your Component Sheet to knoll the parts. Grouping one servo horn with its two legs and hardware will prevent the "left" and "right" assemblies from getting mixed up.
g.title: Walker Disassembly: Step 4

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint for Step 5 showing the removal of a grommet from a robot leg.
The Action: A single wooden Leg is shown with a circular Grommet being detached from the rounded mounting hole at the bottom (the "foot" area).
The Connection: An orange dashed arrow points downward and away from the semi-circular notch in the leg, indicating that the grommet slides out of the bottom of the assembly.
Text Prompt: "Remove Grommet from each Leg. Repeat 4x."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Friction Fit: The grommet is a soft, spool-shaped component that stays in place via friction and its flanged edges. To remove it, apply gentle pressure to the center of the grommet. If it is stuck, you can slightly compress the rubber or plastic material to help it clear the wooden edges of the leg.
Material Inspection: Since these grommets act as the contact point or "shoe" for the robot, check them for any flattened spots or debris. Cleaning them now with a damp cloth will ensure better traction when the Walker is reassembled.
Small Part Warning: These are among the smallest non-hardware items in the kit. Once they are removed from the four legs, place them in a small container or a designated area on your Component Sheet so they don't roll off your workspace.
g.title: Walker Disassembly: Step 5

g.desc:
The Visual: A blue header section contains the bold text "DISASSEMBLY COMPLETE!" in all caps. Below this, a large white rounded rectangle contains the final instructions in blue text.
Text Prompt: "If not already done, place each piece on its spot on the Components sheet."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Final Inventory Audit: Now that all components are separated, compare your parts against the Inventory List we used at the start. If you find you are missing a small #4-40 hex nut or a grommet, check the floor or the crevices of your workspace before clearing the area.
Organizing for Next Time: When placing items on the Component Sheet, ensure each piece is oriented correctly within its designated outline. Grouping your brass hardware by type (A, B, C, and D) now will save you a significant amount of time during the assembly phase.
Surface Check: Before you put the wooden pieces away, take a moment to feel the edges. If any of the laser-cut plywood has developed minor splinters or char residue during use, a very light touch of fine-grit sandpaper can smooth it out without affecting the fit.
g.title: Walker Disassembly Complete

text.tspan: piece on its spot on the
